title: Longo urges super industry cold-call ban
full_text:
Departing ASIC chairman Joe Longo says unlicensed lead generators must be banned from talking to consumers about superannuation products, giving his strong backing to a government push to overhaul the rules for the murky industry.
In his last keynote speech as head of the Australian Securities and Investments Commission, to be delivered on Thursday, Longo will argue that allowing lead generators to cold call consumers about their retirement savings is the equivalent of letting "someone perform heart surgery just because they've watched a lot of ER".
"We must disrupt the lead generation model that has enabled this conveyor belt of consumer harm to occur," he will tell the Financial Counselling Australia Conference in Cairns.
Longo says prohibiting any unlicensed communications about super was a necessary precaution to prevent the next Shield and First Guardian-style collapses, in which lead generators and dodgy financial advisers convinced thousands of victims to invest $1 billion in the two defective schemes.
"We have witnessed unscrupulous actors trying to exploit the significant pot of money in our superannuation system through what we suspect is industrial-scale misconduct," he will say. "The most effective way to address this misconduct is to stop it at the source, and a ban on unlicensed communications would certainly go a long way to achieving that."
Longo's comments are the most forceful yet supporting Assistant Treasurer Daniel Mulino's push to reform the lead generation sector.
A Treasury consultation paper released last month raised the prospect of licensing the sector, such as financial advisers, or even banning anyone without a financial services or advice licence from talking to consumers about their superannuation at all.
The outgoing ASIC chairman, whose term ends on May 31, said there would "always be business models emerging that test the regulatory perimeter".
In the case of Shield and First Guardian, the lead generators got around anti-hawking laws that apply to financial products by advertising a "free super check" on social media.
Consumers would provide their details after clicking on the ad, consenting to the call from the lead generator, who would then aggressively push them to switch super funds and invest in these schemes.
ASIC earlier this year commenced a review into the lead generation sector and warned consumers that these actors often employ misleading tactics to generate interest in their products.
summary: Outgoing ASIC chairman Joe Longo advocates for a ban on unlicensed lead generators from contacting consumers about superannuation to prevent consumer harm and misconduct in the sector.

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: Joe Longo supports banning unlicensed communications regarding superannuation.
He cites past failures like Shield and First Guardian as examples of consumer exploitation.
A Treasury consultation paper suggests potential licensing reforms for the lead generation sector.
relevance: This issue is pertinent to actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts regulatory practices and consumer trust in financial products.
